title: Deddf y Sector Amaethyddol (Cymru) 2014 / Agricultural Sector (Wales) Act 2014
short: Deddf y Sector Amaethyddol / Agricultural Sector Act 2014
type: Primary Legislation — Senedd Cymru / Welsh Parliament (enacted as Emergency Bill)
chapters: 19 adran / sections. An emergency Act passed in response to the UK Government's abolition of the Agricultural Wages Board for England and Wales (ERRA 2013 s.72). The Welsh Assembly (now Senedd Cymru) legislated to PRESERVE agricultural worker protections in Wales. The UK Attorney General challenged the Assembly's competence — the Supreme Court ruled in Wales's favour. This Act is therefore both an EMPLOYMENT PROTECTION instrument and a CONSTITUTIONAL LANDMARK in Welsh devolution. It establishes the Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales, provides for agricultural wages orders setting minimum rates ABOVE the NMW/NLW, and promotes careers and skills in the agricultural sector.
bilingualNote: Mae'r Ddeddf hon ar gael yn Gymraeg ac yn Saesneg. / This Act is available in Welsh and English. The Agricultural Wages Orders are published bilingually. Key terms: gweithiwr amaethyddol (agricultural worker), amaethyddiaeth (agriculture), Panel Cynghori Amaethyddol Cymru (Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales), Gorchymyn Cyflogau Amaethyddol (Agricultural Wages Order).

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Bil y Sector Amaethyddol (Cymru) / Agricultural Sector (Wales) Bill — EMERGENCY BILL
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Alun Davies AM, Gweinidog Adnoddau Naturiol a Bwyd / Minister for Natural Resources and Food, 8 July 2013 (Emergency Bill procedure — Standing Order 26.95)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementDate: 2014-07-30 (all provisions in force on Royal Assent — s.19)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: All provisions came into force on the day of Royal Assent (30 July 2014). This was an EMERGENCY BILL — the Welsh Assembly acted urgently because the UK Government's abolition of the Agricultural Wages Board (ERRA 2013 s.72) was already in effect (1 October 2013), leaving Welsh agricultural workers without the protection of agricultural minimum wages. The Act filled this gap by preserving equivalent protections under Welsh devolved authority. The Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales was established by Order on 1 April 2016.

precedingDocuments.title: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 (ERRA), s.72 — Abolition of the Agricultural Wages Board
precedingDocuments.significance: The ERRA 2013 abolished the Agricultural Wages Board for England and Wales. In England, agricultural workers fell back to the standard National Minimum Wage. The Welsh Assembly legislated to preserve HIGHER agricultural minimum wages in Wales.

precedingDocuments.title: Agricultural Wages Board for England and Wales — Agricultural Wages (England and Wales) Order 2012
precedingDocuments.significance: The LAST Order made by the Agricultural Wages Board before its abolition. The 2014 Act (s.10) preserved the terms of this Order in Wales as transitional provisions until new Agricultural Wages (Wales) Orders were made.

precedingDocuments.title: Attorney General v National Assembly for Wales [2014] UKSC 43 — SUPREME COURT CONSTITUTIONAL RULING
precedingDocuments.significance: The UK Attorney General (Dominic Grieve) referred the Agricultural Sector (Wales) Bill to the Supreme Court, arguing it was OUTSIDE the Assembly's legislative competence because it related to employment (a reserved matter). The SUPREME COURT ruled in the Assembly's favour — holding that the Bill related to AGRICULTURE (a devolved matter), not employment per se. This was a landmark ruling on the scope of Welsh devolution. It confirmed that the Welsh Assembly could legislate on matters that AFFECT employment, provided the legislation relates to a devolved subject matter (agriculture).

leadingCases.name: Attorney General v National Assembly for Wales Commission
leadingCases.court: Supreme Court of the United Kingdom
leadingCases.principle: The Supreme Court held that the Agricultural Sector (Wales) Bill was within the legislative competence of the National Assembly for Wales. The Bill related to AGRICULTURE (a devolved matter under the Government of Wales Act 2006), not to EMPLOYMENT (a reserved matter). The fact that the Bill had EFFECTS on employment did not take it outside the Assembly's competence, because its PURPOSE was to regulate the agricultural sector — which included the terms and conditions of agricultural workers.
leadingCases.heldText: The Bill relates to agriculture, which is a subject within the Assembly's legislative competence. The provision for minimum wages for agricultural workers is a provision about the agricultural sector, not about employment law in general.
leadingCases.significance: This is one of the most important Welsh devolution cases. It confirmed that the boundary between devolved and reserved matters is determined by the SUBJECT MATTER of the legislation (agriculture), not its EFFECTS (employment). This principle has implications for all Welsh legislation that touches employment — including the Social Partnership Act 2023 and the Well-being of Future Generations Act 2015.

parts.title: Rhan 1 / Part 1 — Panel Cynghori Amaethyddol Cymru a Gorchmynion Cyflogau / The Advisory Panel and Agricultural Wages Orders

sections.title: Y Panel, y Gorchmynion Cyflogau, a'r pum gradd o weithwyr / The Panel, Wages Orders, and the five grades of workers
sections.summary: The Act establishes the Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales and provides for agricultural wages orders that set minimum rates of pay, allowances, and terms of employment for agricultural workers in Wales. The Panel consists of 7 members representing farmers, trade unions, and independent expertise. It drafts annual wages orders, consults on proposed changes, and submits them to Welsh Ministers for approval. The orders are passed by the Senedd and have legal authority.
sections.text:
PANEL CYNGHORI AMAETHYDDOL CYMRU / AGRICULTURAL ADVISORY PANEL FOR WALES (s.2):

The Welsh Ministers must establish an Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales. The Panel was created by the Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales (Establishment) Order 2016, operational from 1 April 2016.

COMPOSITION (7 members):
(a) Chair (independent, appointed through Public Appointment Process).
(b) 1 representative of Undeb Amaethwyr Cymru / Farmers' Union of Wales (FUW).
(c) 1 representative of NFU Cymru / National Farmers' Union Cymru.
(d) 1 representative of UNITE the Union (worker representative).
(e) 3 independent members (appointed through Public Appointment Process).

The tripartite structure mirrors the Social Partnership Council — employer representatives (farming unions), worker representative (UNITE), and independent members providing expert judgment.

FUNCTIONS:
(a) Preparing AGRICULTURAL WAGES ORDERS in draft.
(b) Consulting on proposed changes to orders.
(c) Submitting draft orders to Welsh Ministers for approval.
(d) Advising Welsh Ministers on matters relating to the agricultural sector.
(e) Promoting CAREERS in agriculture (unique — goes beyond wages).
(f) Identifying SKILLS NEEDS in the agricultural sector.

GORCHMYNION CYFLOGAU AMAETHYDDOL / AGRICULTURAL WAGES ORDERS (ss.3-4):

The Panel drafts annual wages orders setting minimum pay rates and employment terms for agricultural workers in Wales.

Process:
(1) Panel meets (typically September) to review current rates and propose changes.
(2) Panel conducts a 4-week CONSULTATION with stakeholders.
(3) Panel submits draft order to Welsh Ministers.
(4) Welsh Ministers approve the order.
(5) Order is laid before and passed by Senedd Cymru.
(6) Order comes into force (typically 1 APRIL each year).

Current order: GORCHYMYN CYFLOGAU AMAETHYDDOL (CYMRU) 2025 / Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order 2025 — in force from 1 April 2025.

Y PUM GRADD O WEITHWYR / THE FIVE GRADES OF AGRICULTURAL WORKERS:

The Order classifies agricultural workers into 5 grades with escalating minimum rates:

(1) GRADD A1 / GRADE A1 — Initial Grade:
New entrants to agriculture with no prior experience. Minimum hourly rate set above the National Living Wage.

(2) GRADD A2 / GRADE A2 — Standard Worker:
Workers with some experience or who have completed basic training.

(3) GRADD A3 / GRADE A3 — Lead Worker:
Experienced workers who may supervise others or operate specialised machinery.

(4) GRADD A4 / GRADE A4 — Craft Grade:
Highly skilled workers with recognised qualifications or substantial experience in specialised areas.

(5) GRADD A5 / GRADE A5 — Management Grade:
Workers with management responsibilities, typically farm managers or senior supervisors.

Apprentices have a separate (lower) minimum rate.

KEY PRINCIPLE: ALL grades are set at or ABOVE the National Minimum Wage / National Living Wage. The agricultural minimum wage is ALWAYS at least equal to, and usually HIGHER than, the NMW/NLW. If the NMW/NLW exceeds any agricultural rate, the NMW/NLW applies automatically (s.10(4)).

ADDITIONAL TERMS IN THE ORDER:

The wages order covers more than just pay. It includes:
(a) GORAMSER / OVERTIME: Rates for hours worked beyond contractual hours. The 2025 Order clarified that overtime is payable once full contractual hours are completed.
(b) TÂL SALWCH / SICK PAY: Agricultural Sick Pay provisions.
(c) GWYLIAU / HOLIDAY: Entitlement and calculation — the 2025 Order updated holiday pay calculation to align with Working Time Regulations 1998 amendments for part-year and irregular-hours workers.
(d) LLETY / ACCOMMODATION: Offset rate where the employer provides accommodation (added to pay if free; deducted from pay if charged).
(e) LWFANS AR ALWAD / ON-CALL ALLOWANCE: Payment for periods when the worker is on-call.
(f) GADAEL HEB DÂL / UNPAID LEAVE: The 2025 Order recognised statutory rights to Carer's Leave (Carer's Leave Regulations 2024) and Parental Leave.
(g) PRENTISIAETH / APPRENTICESHIP: Framework for agricultural apprenticeships with specific pay rates.
(h) PLANT / CHILDREN: Restrictions on child labour in agriculture — minimum age 13 (or 14 in some areas).

WHY WELSH AGRICULTURAL WORKERS ARE BETTER PROTECTED THAN ENGLISH:

Since the ERRA 2013 abolished the Agricultural Wages Board:

— In ENGLAND: agricultural workers receive the standard NMW/NLW only. No sector-specific minimum wages. No grading system. No agricultural sick pay. No agricultural overtime rates. Employment terms are governed by individual contracts and UK-wide legislation.

— In WALES: agricultural workers receive sector-specific minimum wages (typically HIGHER than NMW/NLW for experienced workers), a grading system recognising skill and responsibility, agricultural sick pay, overtime rates, and employment terms set by the Agricultural Wages Order. The Panel reviews rates ANNUALLY and consults with both farming unions and trade unions.

This creates a CROSS-BORDER DIFFERENTIAL. An employer with farms in both England and Wales must pay different minimum rates to agricultural workers on each side of the border.

WHO IS AN 'AGRICULTURAL WORKER'?

The Act defines agriculture broadly to include:
— Farming (arable, livestock, dairy, horticulture).
— Forestry (coedwigaeth).
— Market gardening.
— Nursery grounds.
— Orchards.
— Allotments.

An 'agricultural worker' (gweithiwr amaethyddol) is a person EMPLOYED IN AGRICULTURE IN WALES — whether or not all of their work is performed in Wales. This means that if a worker is employed by a Welsh farm but works partly in England, the Welsh agricultural minimum wage still applies.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Agricultural Sector (Wales) Act preserves sector-specific employment protections for agricultural workers in Wales that no longer exist in England. The annual wages order sets minimum rates ABOVE the NMW/NLW, recognises 5 grades of worker skill, and provides terms (sick pay, overtime, holiday, accommodation) that go beyond UK-wide minimums. For employers with operations in both England and Wales, this creates a cross-border differential that must be managed. The Act is also a constitutional landmark — the Supreme Court's ruling confirmed the scope of Welsh devolution.
refs: Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order 2025 (wsi/2025/293) — current Order, in force 1 April 2025
Agricultural Advisory Panel for Wales (Establishment) Order 2016
ERRA 2013 s.72 — abolition of Agricultural Wages Board
Attorney General v National Assembly for Wales [2014] UKSC 43

regulatoryGuidance.title: Agricultural Minimum Wage 2025 to 2026 — guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Annual guidance from the Welsh Government on the current agricultural wages order, including pay rates, allowances, and terms for all 5 grades.

commonErrors: Not knowing that agricultural workers in Wales have HIGHER minimum wages than those in England — since 2013, English agricultural workers receive only the NMW/NLW. Welsh agricultural workers receive sector-specific minimums that are typically higher, especially for experienced workers (Grades A3-A5)

Not applying the correct grade to workers — the 5-grade system (A1 initial to A5 management) recognises different skill levels and responsibilities. Paying all workers the Grade A1 rate when they should be on A3 or A4 is underpayment and a breach of the Order

Assuming the Order only covers pay — it also covers overtime rates, sick pay, holiday entitlement and calculation, accommodation offset, on-call allowances, and unpaid leave. Employers must comply with ALL terms, not just the minimum hourly rate
Not updating rates annually — the wages order is reviewed and updated EVERY YEAR (typically in force from 1 April). Employers must check the current rates each April and update payroll accordingly

Not knowing who qualifies as an 'agricultural worker' — the definition is BROAD. It includes farming, forestry, horticulture, market gardening, nurseries, and orchards. A worker employed in any of these activities in Wales is covered by the Order

Applying NMW/NLW rates to Welsh agricultural workers instead of the agricultural minimum wage — the agricultural rate applies if it is HIGHER than the NMW/NLW. If the NMW/NLW exceeds the agricultural rate for a particular grade, the NMW/NLW applies as a floor

Not understanding the cross-border differential — an employer with farms in England and Wales must pay different minimum rates. Welsh agricultural workers are entitled to the Welsh agricultural minimum wage even if the employer is based in England

Not knowing about the Supreme Court ruling — the 2014 Act is constitutionally significant. The Supreme Court confirmed that the Welsh Assembly could legislate on agricultural workers' terms because the legislation related to AGRICULTURE (devolved), not employment per se (reserved). This principle underpins all Welsh legislation that touches employment

versions.text: IN FORCE: all provisions from Royal Assent. Panel established 1 April 2016. Current wages order: Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order 2025 (in force 1 April 2025). Orders updated annually.
versions.amendedBy: Annual Agricultural Wages (Wales) Orders — most recently the 2025 Order (wsi/2025/293)
versions.policyRationale: Emergency legislation to fill the gap created by the abolition of the Agricultural Wages Board. The Welsh Government chose to PRESERVE and ENHANCE agricultural worker protections rather than allow them to fall to the NMW/NLW floor.

pendingAmendments.status: The Panel will review rates in September 2025 and consult on proposed changes. The 2026 Order is expected to come into force 1 April 2026. Monitor for updated rates.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — rates change annually and employers must update payroll

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 interaction — Day-One rights for agricultural workers
pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025's Day-One unfair dismissal rights (from January 2027) will apply to agricultural workers in Wales alongside the Welsh agricultural minimum wage protections. This strengthens the combined protection for agricultural workers.

pendingAmendments.instrument: SPPP Act — agricultural contracts with Welsh public bodies
pendingAmendments.status: Where Welsh public bodies procure agricultural services, the SPPP Act's fair work and socially responsible procurement duties will interact with the agricultural minimum wage. Monitor for guidance on how agricultural wages orders relate to fair work clauses.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: LOW — niche interaction but relevant for public sector agricultural contracts

surveillanceStatus.status: IN FORCE — mature framework (since 2014), annual wages orders, Supreme Court confirmed competence
